experience: Attending the 'Ndocciata torchlight procession and communal roasted chestnut ritual in Agnone on the night of December 8th (Feast of the Immaculate Conception), where locals carry enormous hand-forged iron torches ('ndocce) weighing up to 50kg through the medieval streets, ending in a bonfire and shared food in the piazza
when: Evening of December 8th annually, and sometimes Christmas Eve
why_distinctive: Molise is Italy's most overlooked region. This 2,000-year-old pagan-Christian fire ritual is performed by an ageing community of ironworkers and is recognized by UNESCO as intangible cultural heritage, yet receives almost no mainstream tourism coverage. The torches are forged in local smithies visitors can tour the same day.
source: Documented in Tim Parks' travel writing on forgotten southern Italy, and substantively covered in Rachel Roddy's 'Two Kitchens' (2017) surrounding Molise food traditions; UNESCO Intangible Heritage nomination file for 'Ndocciata (2019)
